---
title: "Architecture"
---

This document contains the architectural design for zen-do. For design
details of the Seedcase Project as a whole, see the
[Seedcase Design](https://design.seedcase-project.org) documentation.

The purpose of this description is to ensure that the team shares a
common understanding about the implementation and to outline the design
to anyone else interested in the internal workings of the package.

## Naming

This section contains a naming scheme and explanation for zen-do for the
main actions and objects used throughout the package.

| Object                  | Description                                                                                                                         |
|-------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| deposit(s)              | A Zenodo deposit is the combined set of files and metadata that make up the content that will eventually form the published record. |
| deposit ID (or just ID) | The unique ID given to a deposit.                                                                                                   |
| record(s)               | A Zenodo record is a given published set of metadata and files for a specific DOI.                                                  |
| metadata                | The metadata for a deposit, kept within a file `.zenodo.json`.                                                                      |
| file(s)                 | The files to add to a deposit.                                                                                                      |
| format                  | The file format for the Zenodo metadata file.                                                                                       |
| token                   | The Zenodo personal access token to authenticate with the Zenodo API.                                                               |

: Objects used throughout zen-do.

| Action  | Description                                                                                                        |
|---------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| get     |                                                                                                                    |
| convert | Convert metadata files to and from different formats.                                                              |
| update  | Update an existing Zenodo deposit from a metadata file, as well as potentially adding new files.                   |
| create  | Create a new Zenodo (draft) deposit from a metadata file and                                                       |
| publish | Generate structured, human-readable output from a Data Package's properties using the configuration and templates. |

: Actions used within Flower, including the CLI commands and Python API functions.

## C4 Models

This section contains diagrams inspired by
[C4 Model](https://c4model.com/) for zen-do. The C4 Model is an
established visualisation approach to describe the architecture of a
software system. It breaks the system down into four levels of
architectural abstraction: system context, containers, components, and
code. For zen-do, we only use diagrams that are similar to the context
and container models.

### System context

The system context diagram shows the users and external systems that
interact with Flower.

The users, described in the [User types](#user-types) section, provide
Flower with their Data Package's metadata to convert it into structured,
human-readable documents.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-c4-context
%%| fig-cap: "Diagram showing the interaction between user,  and external systems Flower interacts with."
flowchart TB
    user("User")
    zen_do["zen-do<br>[CLI]"]
    metadata("Metadata file<br>['.zenodo.toml']")
    metadata_formats("Metadata formats<br>[e.g. 'CITATION.cff',<br>'.zenodo.json',<br>'pyproject.toml']")
    deposit("Deposit<br>[Zenodo server]")
    record("Record<br>[Zenodo server]")
    file("File<br>[e.g. PDF]")

    user -->|edit| metadata
    metadata -->|read| zen_do
    metadata <-.-> metadata_formats
    user --> zen_do
    zen_do -->|convert| metadata_formats
    file -->|add| zen_do
    user --> file
    zen_do -->|create<br>update<get>| deposit
    zen_do -->|publish| record
    deposit -.-> record

    %% Styling
    style user fill:#FFFFFF, color:#000000
    style deposit fill:#FFFFFF, color:#000000
    style record fill:#FFFFFF, color:#000000
    style metadata_formats fill:#FFFFFF, color:#000000
```

### Container

In C4, a [container diagram](https://c4model.com/diagrams/container)
zooms in on the system boundary to show the *containers* within it, such
as web applications or databases. This diagram displays the main
containers of Flower, their responsibilities, and how they interact,
including the technologies used for each.

Flower is primarily designed for use as a command-line interface (CLI),
with its core functionality also exposed as a Python API.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-c4-container
%%| fig-cap: "C4 container diagram showing the core Python package in Flower and the command line interface."
flowchart

    user("User<br>[person]")
    metadata("Metadata<br>[toml]")
    metadata_formats("Metadata formats<br>[json, toml, cff]")
    id
    token

    subgraph "zen-do"
        get
        convert
        create
        update
        publish
    end

    %% Styling
    style zen-do fill:#FFFFFF, color:#000000
```

### Component/code

In the diagram below, we zoom in on the core Python package container to
show its internal components. In C4, a
[component](https://c4model.com/abstractions/component) is "a grouping
of related functionality encapsulated behind a well-defined interface",
like a class or a module, while
[code](https://c4model.com/abstractions/code) is the basic building
blocks, such as classes and functions.

Because the core Python package is relatively small and simple, and
because both component and code diagrams include classes, we combine the
component and code levels of the C4 model into a single diagram as shown
below. This diagram shows the main classes and functions within the
Python package that represents the CLI.

<!-- TODO: Switch to using a Properties class instead of dict? -->

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-c4-component
%%| fig-cap: "C4 component diagram showing the parts of the Python package that contribute to the CLI and their connections."
flowchart TB
    subgraph package["Python code"]
        subgraph cfg["Configuration"]
            config("Config<br>[class]")
            section("Section<br>[class]")
        end

        read_properties["read_properties()<br>[function]"]
        properties["properties<br>[dict]"]
        build("build()<br>[function]")
        view("view()<br>[function]")

        read_properties --> properties
        properties --> build & view
        cfg --> build & view
    end

    user("User<br>[person]")
    templates("Templates<br>[folder with<br>Jinja files]")
    output_files("Output<br>[files and<br>folders]")
    output_screen("Output<br>[text in the<br>Terminal]")

    user --"Gives<br>datapackage.json"--> read_properties
    user --"Gives templates<br>in folder<br>(optional)"--> templates
    user --"Gives configurations,<br>as file, Python classes,<br>or from CLI<br>(optional)"--> config
    templates --"Sets output<br>structure and layout"--> section
    build --"Populates template(s)<br>with metadata items"--> output_files
    view --"Displays output<br>to screen"--> output_screen

    %% Styling
    style package fill:#FFFFFF, color:#000000
    style cfg fill:#FFFFFF, color:#000000, stroke-dasharray: 5 5
```

For more details on the individual classes and functions, see the
[interface documentation](/docs/design/interface/index.qmd) and the
[reference documentation](/docs/reference/index.qmd).
